content.text: Why Standard ADR Templates Fall Short for GenAI

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) — popularised by Michael Nygard's format of context, decision, status, and consequences — work well for conventional software architecture decisions. They struggle with GenAI decisions because the decision space is qualitatively different: choices are probabilistic rather than deterministic, consequences include emergent quality degradation rather than just system failures, and decisions often encode implicit bets on provider roadmaps that must be made explicit.

A standard ADR template asks 'what did we decide and why?' A GenAI ADR must additionally capture: what quality threshold this decision assumes, what observability will detect if the assumption proves wrong, what the reversal cost is (because changing a model or switching from RAG to fine-tuning mid-project is expensive), and which compliance or regulatory constraints the decision satisfies. Without these extensions, ADRs for GenAI systems become outdated faster and provide less guidance when the original authors are unavailable.

attrs.alt: ADR lifecycle states and GenAI-specific decision categories

content.text: The GenAI ADR Template
The following template extends the standard Nygard format with five GenAI-specific sections. Every production GenAI ADR should use this format.

# ADR-[NUMBER]: [Short Title — e.g., 'Use RAG over Fine-tuning for FNOL Document Retrieval']

**Status:** [Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded by ADR-NNN | Rejected]
**Date:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Deciders:** [names and roles of decision participants]
**Review date:** YYYY-MM-DD  <!-- set to 6 months from acceptance for model-dependent decisions -->

---

## Context

Describe the forces at play — business requirements, technical constraints, the current state
of the system, and the problem this decision addresses. Write for a reader who was not in the
room. Include:
- The user or business scenario driving this decision
- Any constraints that are non-negotiable (compliance, cost ceiling, latency SLO)
- The state of the technology landscape at the time of decision (model capabilities,
  provider availability, cost per token) — this dates the decision correctly

## Decision

State the decision in one sentence. Then explain why this option was chosen over the
alternatives considered. Be precise: name the model version, provider, library version,
or configuration parameter where relevant. Do not write "we will use an LLM" — write
"we will use gpt-4o (2024-11-20 version) via the Azure OpenAI Service endpoint in
West Europe, called through the openai Python SDK 1.54.3."

## Alternatives Considered

For each alternative, describe it briefly and explain why it was not chosen.
Do not write one-line dismissals — explain the real tradeoff.

| Alternative | Why not chosen |
|-------------|----------------|
| [Option A]  | [Specific reason — cost, latency, accuracy gap, compliance, etc.] |
| [Option B]  | [Specific reason] |

## Quality Assumptions

List the measurable assumptions this decision depends on. If any assumption is violated
in production, this ADR should be revisited.

- Accuracy: [e.g., "FNOL triage accuracy >= 0.85 on the held-out eval set"]
- Latency: [e.g., "P95 end-to-end < 5 000 ms at 20 RPS sustained load"]
- Cost: [e.g., "Cost per request < $0.015 at production token volumes"]
- Availability: [e.g., "Provider SLA >= 99.9% monthly uptime"]

## Consequences

### Positive consequences
- [Benefit 1]
- [Benefit 2]

### Negative consequences / accepted risks
- [Risk 1 — include likelihood and severity]
- [Risk 2]

### Technical debt incurred
- [Shortcut taken — describe what must be revisited and by when]

## Observability

How will we know if this decision is working as expected — or has stopped working?

- Metric: [name] | Target: [value] | Alert threshold: [value]
- Metric: [name] | Target: [value] | Alert threshold: [value]

## Reversal cost

Estimate the cost of undoing this decision. Include:
- Developer effort (person-days)
- Data migration or re-embedding effort
- Re-evaluation effort (eval dataset, re-running evals)
- Downtime or migration risk

[Low | Medium | High | Very High] — [1-sentence justification]

## Compliance and governance

- Regulatory: [what regulation this decision satisfies or implicates]
- Privacy: [how PII is handled under this decision]
- Audit: [what audit trail this decision requires]
- Sign-off: [who approved this decision — DPO, legal, security, etc.]

## Links

- Supersedes: ADR-NNN (if applicable)
- Superseded by: ADR-NNN (if applicable, after deprecation)
- Related: [ADR-NNN, ticket, design doc, RFC]
Worked Example: Model Provider Selection ADR
The following is a completed ADR for the FNOL claim chatbot model provider decision. It demonstrates how each section should be filled in at the level of specificity required.

# ADR-001: Use Azure OpenAI gpt-4o for FNOL Claim Triage and Drafting

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2024-11-15
**Deciders:** Alice Chen (Principal Architect), Bob Okafor (Legal), Carol Diaz (DPO),
              David Park (Product), Eve Muller (Platform Lead)
**Review date:** 2025-05-15

---

## Context

InsureCo processes 4 200 FNOL (First Notice of Loss) claims per day. Claim handlers
spend an average of 22 minutes per claim on initial triage and drafting acknowledgement
letters. The AI Claims project aims to reduce this to 8 minutes by automating:
1. Peril classification (flood, fire, theft, vehicle damage)
2. Urgency scoring (critical / standard / low)
3. First-draft acknowledgement letter in InsureCo tone

**Non-negotiable constraints:**
- All PII and claim data must stay within the EU (GDPR data residency)
- No customer data may be used to train or fine-tune provider models
- Latency P95 must be < 5 000 ms to avoid claim handler workflow disruption
- Monthly API cost must be < £8 000 at full production volume (4 200 req/day)

**Technology landscape at decision date (2024-11-15):**
- GPT-4o (OpenAI) — highest accuracy on claims classification tasks in internal eval
- GPT-4o-mini — 75% lower cost; 12% lower accuracy on our eval set
- Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic via AWS Bedrock) — comparable accuracy; no EU region
  available at this date
- Gemini 1.5 Pro (Google Vertex) — untested; no insurance domain fine-tuning

## Decision

We will use **gpt-4o (version: 2024-11-20)** through **Azure OpenAI Service, deployed
in the West Europe region (Netherlands)**, called via the openai Python SDK (>=1.54.3).
Prompts are versioned in the fnol-prompts repository; model version is pinned in
application config (AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME=fnol-gpt4o-2024-11-20).

For cost control, requests are routed by a triage classifier:
- Short inputs (<800 tokens, peril classification only): gpt-4o-mini
- Long inputs or full letter drafting: gpt-4o

## Alternatives Considered

| Alternative | Why not chosen |
|-------------|----------------|
| GPT-4o-mini (all requests) | 12% accuracy drop on flood/liability edge cases; rejection rate rises from 4% to 19% on eval — unacceptable claim handler rework volume |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (AWS Bedrock) | No EU region available at decision date; GDPR data residency requirement cannot be met |
| Self-hosted Llama 3.1 70B | GPU infrastructure cost £45k/month at required throughput; accuracy 18% below gpt-4o on claims eval; 6-month setup timeline incompatible with project schedule |
| Azure OpenAI with no version pin | Silent model updates caused 8% quality drop in comparable project (ADR-000 post-mortem); version pinning is non-negotiable |

## Quality Assumptions

- Classification accuracy >= 0.87 F1 on held-out eval set (420 items, stratified by peril)
- Letter quality score >= 0.80 on LLM-judge rubric (tone, completeness, accuracy)
- P95 latency < 5 000 ms at 20 RPS sustained (load-tested 2024-11-10)
- Provider uptime >= 99.9% monthly (Azure SLA for PTU deployment tier)
- Cost per request < £0.014 with model-tier routing (validated against token profiles)

## Consequences

### Positive consequences
- Achieves 0.89 F1 on internal eval — exceeds 0.87 target
- EU data residency satisfied via West Europe Azure region
- Provider DPA signed; no training-data clause confirmed in writing
- Cost at 4 200 req/day = £6 200/month — within £8 000 ceiling with routing

### Negative consequences / accepted risks
- Provider lock-in: migrating to a different provider requires re-embedding corpus,
  re-running evals, and re-testing latency — estimated 6 weeks effort (Medium risk)
- Model deprecation: Azure announces 12-month deprecation windows; ADR review date
  set to 6 months to catch deprecation notices early (Low risk with mitigation)
- Regional outage: West Europe outage routes to fallback (static FAQ mode); loss of
  AI capability for duration of outage (Low probability, High severity)

### Technical debt incurred
- Model-tier routing logic is a hand-rolled classifier; should be replaced with an
  evaluator-guided router by Q2 2025 if routing accuracy falls below 0.95

## Observability

- Metric: `classification_f1_p50` | Target: > 0.87 | Alert: < 0.82 for 2 hours
- Metric: `letter_quality_score_p50` | Target: > 0.80 | Alert: < 0.75 for 2 hours
- Metric: `request_latency_p95_ms` | Target: < 5 000 | Alert: > 4 500 for 5 minutes
- Metric: `cost_per_request_gbp` | Target: < 0.014 | Alert: > 0.018 (sustained 15 min)

## Reversal cost

**High** — Switching providers requires: re-testing latency (5 days), re-running eval
suite on new model (3 days), updating all prompt versions for new tokeniser behaviour
(10 days), re-validating data residency and DPA with legal (15 days). Total: ~33 person-days
plus potential 2-week downtime risk during migration.

## Compliance and governance

- GDPR data residency: West Europe Azure region; confirmed in writing with Azure account team
- DPA: Signed DPA with Microsoft (Azure OpenAI addendum) prohibits model training on customer data
- Audit trail: All requests logged with request_id, model_version, input_token_count (no content);
  PII scrubbing applied before log write; 90-day retention then deletion
- Sign-off: DPO (Carol Diaz) 2024-11-14; Legal (Bob Okafor) 2024-11-14; CISO 2024-11-15

## Links

- Related: ADR-002 (context window strategy), ADR-006 (RAG vs fine-tuning)
- Post-mortem referenced: ADR-000 (model update quality incident, 2024-09)
ADR Numbering and Storage
ADR organisation directly affects how useful they are. Poor organisation means decisions are found by accident rather than by search, and superseded decisions are mistaken for current ones.
File Naming Convention

docs/adr/
  ADR-001-model-provider-selection.md
  ADR-002-context-window-strategy.md
  ADR-003-rag-architecture.md
  ADR-004-guardrail-approach.md
  ADR-005-eval-framework-selection.md
  ...
  README.md          # index with one-line summaries and status of all ADRs

Every ADR lives in version control alongside the code it governs. The README.md in the adr/ directory is the first place a new team member reads — it must be kept current whenever an ADR's status changes.
ADR Index Template

# Architecture Decision Records — FNOL Claim Chatbot

| ADR | Title | Status | Date | Review date |
|-----|-------|--------|------|-------------|
| ADR-001 | Model provider selection | Accepted | 2024-11-15 | 2025-05-15 |
| ADR-002 | Context window strategy | Accepted | 2024-11-20 | 2025-05-20 |
| ADR-003 | RAG over fine-tuning for document retrieval | Accepted | 2024-12-01 | 2025-06-01 |
| ADR-004 | Input + output guardrail approach | Accepted | 2024-12-05 | 2025-06-05 |
| ADR-005 | Eval framework: LLM-judge over manual scoring | Accepted | 2024-12-10 | 2025-06-10 |
| ADR-006 | Streaming vs batch response delivery | Accepted | 2025-01-08 | 2025-07-08 |
| ADR-007 | Prompt versioning in Git | Accepted | 2025-01-10 | — |
| ADR-008 | gpt-4o-mini for triage routing | Proposed | 2025-01-15 | — |

## Decision graph

ADR-001 → ADR-002 (context strategy depends on model choice)
ADR-001 → ADR-003 (RAG feasibility depends on embedding model)
ADR-003 → ADR-005 (eval framework covers retrieval quality)
ADR-004 → ADR-007 (guardrail prompts are versioned like model prompts)
GenAI-Specific ADR Categories and Key Questions
Category
Typical ADRs
Key questions to answer
Model selection
Provider, model tier, version pinning, fine-tune vs prompt
What accuracy does this model achieve on our eval set? What is the deprecation policy? Is data residency satisfied?
Architecture pattern
RAG vs fine-tuning, agentic vs pipeline, streaming, caching
What is the reversal cost if the chosen pattern underperforms? What eval metrics detect failure?
Context strategy
Context window budget, history truncation, retrieval chunk size
How does this strategy behave when user sessions grow long? What is the failure mode when context overflows?
Guardrails and safety
Input filtering, output validation, human-in-loop scope
What is the false-positive rate for the guardrail? What is the user experience when a guardrail fires?
Evaluation framework
Scorer type, eval dataset management, CI gate
Who maintains the eval dataset? How are new failure cases added? What score triggers a model rollback?
Operational decisions
Observability stack, cost controls, deployment strategy
What is the cost ceiling? How is a provider outage handled? Who gets paged for a quality regression?
When to Write an ADR
Any decision that is hard to reverse (model provider, architecture pattern, eval framework)
Any decision that involves a compliance or regulatory commitment
Any decision where reasonable engineers on the team disagree about the right approach
Any decision that was informed by a PoC finding or evaluation result
Any decision where the consequences of getting it wrong are felt more than 3 months later

A decision that takes less than 5 minutes to undo does not need an ADR. A decision that takes more than a week to undo always does. When uncertain: write the ADR. The cost of writing an unnecessary ADR is an afternoon; the cost of discovering a missing one during a production incident is weeks.
ADR Review Cadence

GenAI ADRs have a shorter useful life than conventional architecture ADRs because model capabilities, costs, and provider policies change rapidly. An ADR accepted in November 2024 about model selection may be obsolete by May 2025 if a superior model tier is released or a competing provider opens an EU region.
ADR type
Recommended review cadence
Trigger for earlier review
Model selection
6 months
Provider deprecation notice, quality regression alert, new model tier release
Architecture pattern (RAG, fine-tuning)
12 months
New retrieval paradigm, significant eval score improvement with alternative
Safety / guardrails
6 months
Red-team finding, regulatory change, new attack vector discovered
Compliance / governance
12 months or per regulatory cycle
Regulatory change, DPA renewal, audit finding
Operational decisions
12 months
Cost increase > 30%, SLO breach pattern, team feedback on runbook gaps

Review does not mean replace. A review may conclude 'decision still correct — no change needed' and update only the review date. The goal is to ensure each ADR reflects a current, intentional choice rather than an outdated default.
Summary

ADR templates for GenAI decisions extend the standard Nygard format with quality assumptions, observability specifications, reversal cost estimates, and compliance records. Every significant architecture decision — model selection, RAG vs fine-tuning, guardrail approach, eval framework, operational controls — should have an ADR written before the decision is implemented.

The worked FNOL example demonstrates the level of specificity required: specific model version, specific region, specific cost threshold, specific alert thresholds, and specific reversal effort estimate. Vague ADRs — 'we will use an LLM from a major provider' — provide no guidance when the original authors are unavailable and no signal when the assumptions underlying the decision have been violated.
